perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to reimburse the Disclosing Party for any and all losses, liabilities, damages, costs, opportunity costs, and direct or indi rect expenses (including reasonable attorney’s fees, profit opportunity returns foregone and any court costs) incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any prima facie breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may reimburse the Disclosing Party for losses, liabilities, damages, costs, and expenses incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, subject to negotiation and mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'agrees to reimburse' with 'may reimburse' and adding 'subject to negotiation and mutual agreement', the obligation to reimburse for all losses is removed, allowing for discretionary reimbursement which may not cover all losses, potentially violating South African contract law regarding damages for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law on Damages for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_citation: Principle of Full Compensation for Breach of Contract (applicable South African case law)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires that the aggrieved party be placed in the position they would have been in had the breach not occurred, which often means full compensation for damages. This modification weakens this right by making reimbursement discretionary and subject to negotiation, potentially allowing the breaching party to avoid full compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Responding to an adverse report - Trademark - Australia ARTICLE 20 May 2022 Responding to an adverse report L LegalVision More Contributor LegalVision, a commercial law firm founded in 2012, combines legal expertise, technology, and operational skills to revolutionize legal services in Australia, New Zealand, and the UK. Beginning as an online legal documents business, LegalVision transitioned to an incorporated legal practice in 2014, and in 2019 introduced a membership model offering unlimited access to lawyers. Expanding internationally in 2021 and 2022, LegalVision aims to provide cost-effective, quality legal services to businesses globally. Explore Firm Details Why your trade mark application may be rejected & how you can rectify problems identified in the adverse examination report. Australia Intellectual Property Sophie Pemberton Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. When you file a trade mark application, an examiner from IP Australia will assess whether your trade mark is fit for registration. If the examiner does not accept your application for trade mark registration, you will receive an adverse examination report detailing the reasons for objecting to your application. Some of the most common grounds for objection are that your trade mark: lacks distinctiveness; contains generic or descriptive terms relative to the goods and services covered by the application; or is substantially identical or confusingly similar to another registered trade mark or pending trade mark application. This article will discuss why your trade mark application may be rejected and how you can rectify the problems identified in the adverse examination report. Reasons Why You May Have Received an Adverse Report Lack of Distinctiveness To be eligible for trade mark registration, your trade mark must be capable of distinguishing your goods and/or services in the marketplace. For instance, the more unique your trade mark is, the greater chance that your trade mark is distinct and memorable to consumers. It will also be easier to allege trade mark infringement if a competitor uses the same or similar trade mark to yours. If your trade mark lacks distinctiveness, it may not be registrable unless you can demonstrate extensive evidence of use. This will illustrate that your trade mark has become a recognisable badge of origin of your business. A trade mark with "strong" distinctiveness might feature invented words or words that bear no connection to your goods and/or services. For example: "Polaroid" is an invented word that has now become a distinct and recognisable trade mark; and "Apple" is a word that was originally unrelated to electronic goods. Both trade marks are memorable and considered 'inherently distinctive'. A trade mark that is likely to receive a refusal on this basis will usually include descriptive or generic terms. This is because IP Australia is likely to decide that these types of terms should be kept freely available for all traders to use without risk of infringement. An exception to this is where a trade mark has essentially adopted a secondary meaning through extensive use and promotion. Provided you can prove that consumers associate the trade mark with your product or service, despite its non-distinctiveness, it may still be possible to achieve registration. Generic Terms An examiner may believe your trade mark contains a generic word that traders should be able to use freely. In this case, they will usually issue an adverse report on the same grounds as a non-distinctiveness objection. Generic terms describe the product or services that your trade mark covers. For example, a trade mark application for "Computer" will be rejected where the goods you offer under this trade mark are computers or electronic devices. IP Australia will not unfairly prevent other traders from being able to describe their goods and services properly. However, it is possib
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates reimbursement for all losses, whereas the modified version makes reimbursement discretionary and subject to negotiation. This contradicts the principle that the aggrieved party should be placed in the position they would have been in had the breach not occurred, as stated in the Law Explanation. The modification weakens the right to full compensation by making it discretionary, which potentially allows the breaching party to avoid complete compensation for damages. While the scraped snippets don't directly address damages for breach of contract, the Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and comparison of original and changed text highlight a significant shift from mandatory to discretionary reimbursement, which undermines the principle of full compensation for losses.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information must not be copied, reproduced, distribut ed, stored digitally or by other means, or passed to others at any time other than in accordance with this Confidentiality Agreement or with the prior written consent of Transnet.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information must not be copied, reproduced, distributed, or passed to others at any time other than in accordance with this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'stored digitally or by other means' it creates an ambiguity and potential loophole related to digital storage of confidential information. This introduces legal risk, particularly concerning the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) in South Africa.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) of South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA mandates specific security safeguards for personal information, including how it is stored digitally. By removing the restriction on digital storage, this clause creates a potential conflict if the Receiving Party's digital storage practices do not comply with POPIA's requirements for securing personal information.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'stored digitally or by other means' from the original text creates a legal loophole concerning the storage of confidential information. The law explanation states that POPIA mandates specific security safeguards for personal information, including how it is stored digitally, and removing this restriction creates a potential conflict if the Receiving Party's digital storage practices do not comply with POPIA's requirements. The perturbation explanation further supports that the omission creates ambiguity and potential legal risk, particularly concerning POPIA. While no specific laws were present in the snippets, the combination of the 'Law Explanation' and 'Perturbation Explanation' clearly indicate a potential weakening of compliance with data protection laws regarding digital storage, which falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information furnished to it. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and copies and extracts thereof' and 'created by or on behalf of the Receiving Party' the changed text only obligates return of the originally furnished confidential information and not copies, extracts, or material created using that information. By removing 'other obligations hereunder' it weakens the agreement, creating legal contradictions with respect to other contractual requirements in South African Law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law – Interpretation of Contractual Obligations
perturbation.law_citation: General principles of contractual interpretation under South African law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African law, contractual terms are interpreted to give effect to the common intention of the parties. Removing the obligation to return 'copies and extracts' and obligations beyond confidentiality introduces ambiguity and undermines the apparent intention of the parties to protect confidential information broadly, including derivative works. The return of confidential information and the continued confidentiality obligations is a core tenet of this agreement, but the full extent of obligations is not present in the altered text.
perturbation.justification: The modification omits 'and copies and extracts thereof' and 'created by or on behalf of the Receiving Party' and 'other obligations hereunder'. This omission weakens the agreement and opens a loophole where the receiving party may not be legally bound to return or protect copies or materials derived from the original confidential information. By removing 'other obligations hereunder' it weakens the agreement, creating legal contradictions with respect to other contractual requirements. Thus a legal contradiction exists.
